coredata.dc:title: A highly selective fluorescence sensing platform for nanomolar Hg(II) detection based on cytosine derived quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Liang
Song, Ting
Wang, Haoqiang
Yuan, Qunhui
Zhou, Shenghai
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by low toxicity and good biocompatibility of biomass derived quantum dot (QD), we herein developed a cytosine derived quantum dot, namely cyt-dot, via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The as-prepared cyt-dot emits blue fluorescence (FL) containing abundant oxygen (20.6at.%) and nitrogen (24.1at.%) contents. The cyt-dot based sensing platform shows exclusive selectivity for Hg(II) while being insensitive towards Fe(III) and Ag(I), which are important interference that usually cannot be ruled out. The detection limit for Hg(II) is of 11nM, which is very close to the guideline value of 10nM allowed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water. In real water sample analyses, the present sensing platform can fulfil satisfied recoveries ranging from 100% to 108%. Besides, the acidity of solution has almost no effect on the sensing performance of the cyt-dot in a pH range of 5–8, suggesting its potential applications in sensing and bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyt-dot
Cytosine
Hg(II)
Label free
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical and Structural Characterizations of the As-prepared Cyt-dot
FL Response of Cyt-dot Towards Heavy Metallic Ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectra of cyt-dot and cytosine, as well as FL excitation/emission spectra of 0.005mg·mL−1 cyt-dot in 10mM PBS. Inset shows photographs of cyt-dot solutions under the irradiation of daylight (left) and a 365nm UV lamp (right). (b) FL spectra of cyt-dot solutions at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Time-dependent FL spectrum of cyt-dot solution (Xe lamp, 150W, λex =340nm).
(a and b) Typical TEM and AFM images of cyt-dot. (c and d) Size distribution and height profile of cyt-dot. Inset in (a) presents the graphitic layered structure in cyt-dot.
(a) FT-IR spectra of cyt-dot and cytosine. (b) XPS spectrum of the cyt-dot. (c and d) High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s and N 1s.
FL response of cyt-dot in the presence of different cations of 20μM. Ccyt-dot =0.005mg·mL−1, 10mM PBS, λex =340nm.

(a) Effect of pH on the quenching efficiency of cyt-dot towards 20μM Hg(II). I0 and I are the normalized FL intensities of cyt-dot in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. (b) Response time of cyt-dot for 20μM Hg(II). Ccyt-dot =0.005mg·mL−1, 10mM PBS, λex =340nm.
(a) Chemical structure of cytosine. (b) FL lifetime profiles of cyt-dot in the absence and presence of 20μM Hg(II). (c) Illustration of FL quenching process.

(a) Normalized FL intensity of cyt-dot in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II). (b) Calibration curve of the normalized FL intensity as a function of Hg(II) concentration. Ccyt-dot =0.005mg·mL−1, 10mM PBS, λex =340nm.
Different fluorescent probes for Hg(II) detection.
Determination of Hg(II) in real water samples (each sample was tested for three times).

head.ce:title: A highly selective fluorescence sensing platform for nanomolar Hg(II) detection based on cytosine derived quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hebei Normal University for Nationalities, Chengde 067000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by low toxicity and good biocompatibility of biomass derived quantum dot (QD), we herein developed a cytosine derived quantum dot, namely cyt-dot, via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The as-prepared cyt-dot emits blue fluorescence (FL) containing abundant oxygen (20.6at.%) and nitrogen (24.1at.%) contents. The cyt-dot based sensing platform shows exclusive selectivity for Hg(II) while being insensitive towards Fe(III) and Ag(I), which are important interference that usually cannot be ruled out. The detection limit for Hg(II) is of 11nM, which is very close to the guideline value of 10nM allowed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water. In real water sample analyses, the present sensing platform can fulfil satisfied recoveries ranging from 100% to 108%. Besides, the acidity of solution has almost no effect on the sensing performance of the cyt-dot in a pH range of 5–8, suggesting its potential applications in sensing and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In past decade, heavy metal pollution has become a critical issue because of its severe harm to human health [1]. Among all heavy metals, Hg(II) attracts intensive attention for its featured high toxicity, good water solubility and trace enrichment in food chains [2]. Many studies have confirmed that the existence and accumulation of Hg(II) would cause permanent damage to the circulatory systems and organs of human beings [3]. Therefore, various kinds of approaches such as atomic absorption spectrometry [4], atomic emission spectrometry [5], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6] have been used in heavy metal analyses with good sensitivities and accuracies. However, sometimes these conventional analytical methods could not meet the demand of rapid determination of trace metals thus impede their practical usages in on-site analyses. Consequently, some relatively low-cost and time-efficient analytical techniques including electrochemical determination [7,8], colorimetric assay [9] fluorescence (FL) analysis [10,11], and so on, have been widely studied.

The determination based on FL signal can fulfil the needs of rapid detection by providing alternative method with high sensitivity, high accuracy and essential simplicity [12]. Thus, varieties of fluorescent probes towards metals have been developed including organic FL molecules [13], metal clusters [14] and semiconductor quantum dots [15]. Owing to the advantages of high emission quantum yields, simple preparation and great chemical stability of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the new sensing platforms based on CQDs have also gained extensive attentions [16]. Since being discovered in 2004, CQDs have been intensively studied in terms of syntheses, properties, and potential applications in fluorescence sensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, devices and so on [17].

Because of their extraordinary FL properties, CQDs have been successfully used as probes for detection of metallic ions such as Ag(I), Hg(II), Cu(II) and Fe(III) in aqueous solutions [18–21]. Compared with the analyses of other cations, reports on the detection of Hg(II) is relatively few due to the low sensitivity [22,23]. This shortcoming in Hg(II) sensing is likely to be overcome by using FL CQDs containing heteroatom dopants (e.g. N, S), which have been found to be superior to the un-doped ones in terms of high FL quantum yield and sensing selectivity [24–26]. For example, Zhang et al. reported a synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) via straight forward hydrothermal strategy by using folic acid as both carbon and nitrogen sources [27]. You et al. successfully synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots by doping L-cysteine and the obtained dots had a high selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) [28]. Zhao et al. developed a facile one-pot solid-phase synthesis strategy for the preparation of nitrogen-doped GQDs. By using citric acid as the carbon source and 3,4-dihy-droxy-l-phenylalanine as the N source, the as-prepared nitrogen-doped GQDs possessed a high selectivity and sensitivity towards Hg(II) [29]. Our group recently demonstrated a new type of DNA derived bio-dots could be used for FL sensing of Hg(II) and Ag(I) [30], due to the formation of cytosine-Ag(I)-cytosine [31] and thymine-Hg(II)-thymine [32] complex.

Although a variety of new FL sensing platforms based on CQDs towards Hg(II) detection have been developed, it is noteworthy there are still two scenarios need to be concerned. First, the independent detection of Hg(II) was still challenging for the non-negligible interference caused by Ag(I), Fe(III) or other cations [28,30]. Second, the hetero-doped CQDs based Hg(II) sensors are sometimes suffering from relatively high detection limits although they are more efficient in selectivity than the un-doped ones. Therefore, the development of CQDs with high selectivity, high sensitivity and low detection limit for Hg(II) is still a pressing desire.

In this extended study, we used a small biomolecule, cytosine, as precursor to develop new FL CQDs for Hg(II) assay. Compared with other biological carbon source such as lotus root [33], biomass [34] and DNA [30], cytosine molecule has well-defined structure and rich contents of N and O, which may lead to the enhanced discrimination accuracies of the derived CQDs for mercury ions. It was found that the cytosine derived CQDs (cyt-dot) exhibited strong FL and were highly selective towards Hg(II). In this respect, the fluorescent property and the selectivity of the cyt-dot are well studied. Moreover, an FL platform based on cyt-dot has also been developed for real water sample analyses.

Chemicals of analytical grade were purchased and used as received. Cytosine (C0528-5G) and Cd(NO3)2·4H2O were purchased from TCI and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively. AgNO3, CoCl2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·12H2O were purchased from Tianjin BaiShi. AlCl3·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, HgCl2, KCl, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2 and ZnCl2 were purchased from Tianjin GuangFu. Deionized water (DI water, ≥18.2MΩ) was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions. 10mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was used to prepare testing solution. The cationic standards with wanted concentrations were obtained by diluting their stock solutions with DI water.

The cyt-dot was synthesized from cytosine via hydrothermal treatment. Briefly, 154mg of cytosine was dissolved in 20mL of DI water at room temperature (~25°C) to get a clean and saturated solution under vigour stirring for 20min. Then, the cytosine solution was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave, heated at 160°C for 4h and cooled down to room temperature naturally. The resulted solution was centrifuged then at 11,000rpm for 3 times of 5min each to get supernatant, which was denoted as cyt-dot supernatant. The cyt-dot supernatant was placed in a 4°C refrigerator for storage. 1μL of the cyt-dot supernatant was diluted with 2mL of 10mM PBS buffer to get a cyt-dot testing solution.

The microstructure and surface morphology of the as-prepared cyt-dot were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi, H-7650), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Siemens D5005) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Multimode 8). The surface contents and functional groups were identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EDAX, Genesis 60S) and fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, BIO-RAD FTS165). The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrometer (Japan). The fluorescence intensity of the testing solution was measured on a Hitachi F-7000 luminescence spectrometer (Japan), with an excitation slit width of 5nm. The fluorescence lifetime was recorded on a time-resolved FluoroLog-3 Modular Spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Jvon, France) with an excitation wavelength of 340nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and Structural Characterizations of the As-prepared Cyt-dot
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a and b shows the UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of the as-prepared cyt-dot. The cyt-dot solution (0.005mg·mL−1) emitted a blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation (inset in Fig. 1a). The UV–vis spectrum of the cyt-dot contains a typical absorption peak at around 260nm, which could be ascribed to the presence of delocalized π electron of the heterocyclic ring [30,35]. The UV–vis adsorption feature of cytosine was also checked. Comparing with that of cytosine, the adsorption peak of the cyt-dot became wider and was red shifted, implying that the transformation from cytosine to cyt-dot was achieved. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1b shows an excitation-independent emission feature as the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 to 360nm, with its emission wavelength fixed at around 378nm. A maximum emission was achieved under excitation at 340nm. The photo-stability was also explored by carrying out a time-dependent photoluminescence survey on the cyt-dot solution. After 2h' irradiation with a Xe lamp of 150W (λex =340nm), the FL intensity of the cyt-dot solution was only decreased by 2.3% (Fig. 1c), indicating that the obtained cyt-dot had good photo-stability.

TEM images in Fig. 2a and b indicated the size of cyt-dot is in a relative narrow range of 1.75–3.75nm. A representative high-resolution TEM image of an individual cyt-dot in Fig. 1a demonstrated that the cyt-dot had a good crystallinity, with a featured lattice spacing (0.221nm) corresponding to the (110) facets of graphite [36]. AFM investigation indicated that the thickness of the cyt-dot was <1.76nm (Fig. 2b, d). These results indicate that the cytosine precursor was transformed into cyt-dot under hydrothermal condition.

Chemical compositions of the obtained cyt-dot and its parenting cytosine were analyzed by FT-IR and XPS. As show in Fig. 3a, similar featured peaks in the spectra of cyt-dot and cytosine were observed, including CO stretching vibration at 1713cm−1 [37], CO deformation vibrations at 1453cm−1 [38]. The peaks around 1662cm−1 and 1235cm−1 are accordance with the vibration of NH2 and CN [30,39], The broad absorption band at 2500 to 3750cm−1 could be assigned to the vibrations of COOH, NH and OH [15,24,40]. These peaks in FT-IR spectra indicated that some functional groups in cytosine might be reserved in the cyt-dot. Besides of the same functional groups shared with cytosine, two new featured peaks for saturated hydrocarbon CH at 2934cm−1 and CNC at 1025cm−1 in the spectrum of cyt-dot were appeared, indicating the formation of new bonds [41].

XPS survey indicate that cyt-dot are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Three predominant peaks at 284.78, 400.36 and 531.53eV in Fig. 3b are associated with C 1s, N 1s and O 1s [24]. The high nitrogen content (24.10at.%) of the obtained cyt-dot was comparable or even higher than some recently reported nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [39,42], implying that this method may provide a concept for the direct preparation of N-CQD via facile and eco-friendly route by using bio-molecule procure. The high resolution O 1s (20.62at.%, Fig. 3c) exhibited three characteristic peaks at 530.9, 531.6 and 532.9eV, which could be assigned to CO, COH, COC [25], respectively. The N 1s peaks (Fig. 3d) at 398.8, 399.9 and 400.05eV could be attributed to NH2, CNC and CN groups [27]. The results from FT-IR and XPS characterizations confirmed the abundant existence of oxygen and nitrogen containing groups on the surface of the cyt-dot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL Response of Cyt-dot Towards Heavy Metallic Ions
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation-independent FL emission, the good photo-stability and the abundant structural functional groups for binding may make the as-prepared cyt-dot a good candidate for fluorescence sensing. Therefore, the feasibility of sensing heavy metallic ions was studied based on the present cyt-dot.

In this regard, fourteen kinds of metal ions including Ag(I), Al(III), Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I), Ni(II), Pb(II) and Zn(II) at a constant concentration of 20μM, were added independently into the cyt-dot solution to check the sensing feasibility. It was found that Hg(II) caused a dramatic fluorescence quenching while other metal barely aroused noticeable PL response of the cyt-dot (Fig. 4 ), indicating that the cyt-dot might be used as promising fluorescence probes for the detection of Hg(II) with high selectivity.

To get optimized conditions for Hg(II) sensing, the pH effect on the quenching behaviors of Hg(II) was also studied since the different solution pH may result in varied quenching efficiency of the QDs, owing to the protonation/deprotonation of the surface functional groups on QDs [32,38,43]. It was shown in Fig. 5a that the present cyt-dot exhibited a relatively stable FL response for 20μM Hg(II) at different pH values, suggesting a solution pH independent detecting property of the present cyt-dot. Therefore, we take the pH of 7 for further investigation. The response time of cyt-dot for Hg(II) detection was also estimated. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of the cyt-dot solution was found to be quenched steeply within about 30s upon the addition of 20μM Hg(II), suggesting that the cyt-dot was highly sensitive towards Hg(II). The good optical properties, the great pH stability and the fast response time indicated the cyt-dot was promising for practical application.

The selectivity of cyt-dot for Hg(II) should be attributed to the better specific affinity between them and Hg(II) than with other metallic ions, especially with Ag(I), although the surface functional groups such as COOH, OH, NH2, and so on, are generally used for the interpretation of selective detection of metallic ions like Hg(II), Ag(I) and Fe(III) [21,28,30]. In analogy analyses of the present study with the precursor of cytosine, our group and Ren et al. developed two types of FL sensing platforms for Hg(II), which were based on DNA derived bio-dots or uric acid derived nanoribbons [18,30]. It is found that the obtained FL bio-dots or nano-ribbons are much more sensitive for Ag(I) and Hg(II) than for other potential interfering ions, however, the existence of Ag(I) would have noticeable interference on the FL response of Hg(II). But in this study, the interference from Ag(I) towards Hg(II) is much less tremendous, indicating the mechanism of the selective detection of Hg(II) by the FL cyt-dot in the study might be different in some respects compared with the previous investigation.

Recently, Barman et al. reports a preparation of blue fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (g-CNQDs) and their application as highly selective and sensitive probes for the detection of mercury ions [44]. The complex formation of Hg(II) with the CNx sheet in the fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots involving π delocalized electrons of the latter, was responsible for the quenching of fluorescence. Based on this interpretation and the above results, we proposed the following quenching mechanism. Upon hydrothermal treatment, the cyt-dot with layered graphitic sheets was formed, along with the formation of FL chromophores via stacking. The hydrothermal treatment also generated new ON bonds, which would provide extended structural graphitic network containing NN linkers based on the interpretation proposed by Guo et al. [35]. This structural NN linker would play an important role for the sole selectivity of cyt-dot for Hg(II) due to the formation of NN/Hg(II) complex [44]. Besides, the high affinity between Hg(II) and N, O electron donor atoms may also synergistically contributed to the FL response of cyt-dost considering the Lewis electronic theory of acid and alkali, based on the electron or energy transfer mechanism [45,46]. In this case, since the FT-IR and XPS characterizations have confirmed the existence of electron donors like carboxyl and amino groups in the as-prepared cyt-dot, the quenching mechanism of electron transfer is quite plausible due to the high affinity between cyt-dot and Hg(II) [45–47].

To further reveal the quenching mechanism, the FL lifetimes of the cyt-dot solution in the absence and presence of 20μM Hg(II) were measured. The corresponding fluorescence decay profiles in Fig. 6c demonstrates that the addition of Hg(II) results in a slight FL lifetime change of the cyt-dot (from 3.51 to 3.94ns). The plot in Fig. 7b exhibits the linear dependence of I/I0 on the concentration of Hg(II), which matches the Stern-Volmer equation in the mechanism of static quenching. These evidences indicate that the quenching of cyt-dot caused by Hg(II) occurs in the static mechanism of electron or energy transfer [48,49].

Encouraged by the strong fluorescence response to Hg(II), the cyt-dot towards different concentrations of Hg(II) ranging from 0 to 30μM were evaluated and recorded. As shown in Fig. 6a, the PL intensity of the cyt-dot gradually decreased as the concentrations of Hg(II) increases. To establish a relationship between the PL intensity and the known contents of Hg(II), the PL responses at 377nm was used for analysis. As shown in Fig. 6b, the correlation equations could be defined as I/I0 =0.993−0.035∗CHg(II) (0 to 0.5μM, R2 =0.993) and I/I0 =0.928−0.023∗CHg(II) (0.5 to 20μM, R2 =0.997), where I and I0 were the PL intensities in the presence and absence of Hg(II), respectively. The estimated detection limit of Hg(II) was about 11nM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was comparable with the guideline value (10nM) allowed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water.

The analytical performance of the Hg(II) sensing platforms based on the present cyt-dot and some recently reported analog CQDs were also compared and the results were summarized in Table 1 . The proposed cyt-dot FL sensor showed lower or comparable linear range and detection limit compared with those of the bio-dots, carbon nanodots, FCDs and N-doped carbon dots, indicating this rapid, simple and efficient FL platform may useful for the monitoring of trace Hg(II) in water.

To evaluate the practical performance of the cyt-dot, the obtained cyt-dot was used for the detection of Hg(II) in real water sample. The water sample was collected in Heaven Lake of Celestial Mountains, Urumqi, Xinjiang. No detectable Hg(II) was found in the original lake water. Then the known quantity of Hg(II) was added into the water sample for further detection. Table 2 show that the known amounts of Hg(II) solutions resulted in satisfying recoveries of 100–108% and RSD of 1–5%, indicating the cyt-dot could be used as a probe for monitoring Hg(II) in real water.

In summary, a new type of fluorescent cyt-dot has been synthesized via a facile, green and low-cost route. The cyt-dot exhibit outstanding selectivity and sensitivity for the detection Hg(II) in aqueous solution with the limited detection of 11nM. This makes the cyt-dot based sensor holds a promising potential to be used as a FL sensor for Hg(II). In addition, this work offers a way for fabricating carbon quantum dots from DNA radical bases and may broaden the application of CQDs in fluorescence sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21473247) and the Scientific Research Foundation for the New Teacher in Harbin Institute of Technology (Shenzhen).

A comparison of colorimetric assays detecting hydrogen peroxide in leaf extracts
Upconversion nanoparticle-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer assay for organophosphorus pesticides

Fabrication of valine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and its use as a novel optical probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+
A novel colorimetric/fluorescence dual-channel sensor based on NBD for the rapid and highly sensitive detection of cysteine and homocysteine in living cells
Copper nanocluster-based fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in water and food stuff
A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe
Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots as nanoprobes for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (II) in surface waters
One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Ions-induced two-photon fluorescence dual-switching for reversible and simultaneous sensing of Cu2+ and Hg2+ based on dual-emitting carbon dot/carbon dot conjugates
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon dots for the detection of Fe3+ ions in cancer cells
Facile synthesis the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for selective fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions
Fabrication of a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot from MOF-derived porous carbon and its application for highly selective fluorescence detection of Fe3+
Ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot as on-off-on fluorescent sensor for detection of chromium(VI) and sulfites
Nanoreactor-confined synthesis and separation of yellow-luminescent graphene quantum dots with a recyclable SBA-15 template and their application for Fe(III) sensing
Facile synthesis of oxygen and sulfur co-doped graphitic carbon nitride fluorescent quantum dots and their application for mercury(II) detection and bioimaging
Rapid exfoliation of layered covalent triazine-based frameworks into N-doped quantum dots for the selective detection of Hg2+ ions
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) ions
One-pot green synthesis of oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their potential application in pH-sensitive photoluminescence and detection of mercury(II) ions
DNA derived fluorescent bio-dots for sensitive detection of mercury and silver ions in aqueous solution
Sensitive and selective detection of silver(I) ion in aqueous solution using carbon nanoparticles as a cheap, effective fluorescent sensing platform
N-1-(2-mercaptoethyl)thymine modification of gold nanoparticles: a highly selective and sensitive colorimetric chemosensor for Hg2+
Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging
Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion
A new class of fluorescent-dots: long luminescent lifetime bio-dots self-assembled from DNA at low temperatures
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
One-pot electrochemical synthesis of carbon dots/TiO2 nanocomposites with excellent visible light photocatalytic activity
On–off–on fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for recognition of chromium(VI) and hydrogen sulfide based on the inner filter effect
One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots from ginkgo leaves and application in biology
A novel turn-on fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of Al(III) in an aqueous solution based on simple electrochemically synthesized carbon dots
Pressure-tuning infrared and Raman microscopy study of the DNA bases: adenine, guanine, cytosine, and thymine
Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological applications
Polymer nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging Hg2+ ions in living cells

Facile bulk production of highly blue fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive sensors for the detection of mercuric and iodide ions in aqueous media
A portable RGB sensing gadget for sensitive detection of Hg2+ using cysteamine-capped QDs as fluorescence probe
Use of surface-modified CdTe quantum dots as fluorescent probes in sensing mercury (II)
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Strongly blue-luminescent N-doped carbogenic dots as a tracer metal sensing probe in aqueous medium and its potential activity towards in situ Ag-nanoparticle synthesis
Synthesis of cellulose-derived carbon dots using acidic ionic liquid as a catalyst and its application for detection of Hg2+
The selectivity of the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots switched by buffer solutions for the detection of multi-metal ions
